content.problemStatement:
You are given a list LLL consisting of NNN **binary** strings, each of length MMM.

You can do the following operation any (possibly zero) number of times:


Swap 222 positions in LiL_iLi‚Äã if the same 222 positions are both 111 currently in Li+1L_{i + 1}Li+1‚Äã.
More formally, in one operation, you can:


Pick 333 integers i,j,ki, j, ki,j,k such that:

1‚â§i<N1 \le i \lt N1‚â§i<N;
1‚â§j<k‚â§M1 \le j \lt k \le M1‚â§j<k‚â§M;
L(i+1,j)=L(i+1,k)=1L_{(i + 1, j)} = L_{(i + 1, k)} = 1L(i+1,j)‚Äã=L(i+1,k)‚Äã=1.


Swap the characters L(i,j)L_{(i, j)}L(i,j)‚Äã and L(i,k)L_{(i, k)}L(i,k)‚Äã.
Count the number of different possible strings L1L_1L1‚Äã after some (possibly zero) operations.

Since the answer may be large, output the answer modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.

Note that L(i,j)L_{(i, j)}L(i,j)‚Äã denotes the jthj^{th}jth character of the ithi^{th}ith binary string in list LLL.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and MMM ‚Äî the number of binary strings, and the size of each string.
The ithi^{th}ith of the next NNN lines contains the ithi^{th}ith string of the list LLL, i.e. LiL_iLi‚Äã.




Output Format
For each test case, output a single integer, the number of unique strings L1L_1L1‚Äã modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.
constraints: The sum of N‚ãÖMN \cdot MN‚ãÖM over all test cases doesn't exceed 10^6.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: You can't change L2L_2L2‚Äã so the only positions you can swap in L1L_1L1‚Äã are the 111st and 222nd.
Thus you can get the string 011011011 by doing 111 operation with i=1,j=1,k=2i = 1, j = 1, k= 2i=1,j=1,k=2 and the string 101101101 by doing no operations. It can be shown, you can't get any other string as L1L_1L1‚Äã.
Test case 222: You can't change L1L_1L1‚Äã at all because you will never get a 111 in L2L_2L2‚Äã regardless of what you do.
Test case 333: It can be shown that all permutations of the initial string L1L_1L1‚Äã are possible.
